experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes known evil player 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending evil on Quest 1. Rejecting also gives minimal extra information beyond standard good play, while approving would risk an immediate failed quest and help evil.
Player 2: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, approving an initial 2-person team is standard unless there is clear suspicion; team [0,3] includes the leader and provides information from the first quest. My vote reveals I am open to early information-gathering rather than stalling, which generally helps Good more than an arbitrary reject on the first proposal.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; player 3 proposed a team including me, which is good for evil because it guarantees an evil member on quest 1. As Assassin in the team voting phase, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing me or my teammate, especially on 2-player quest 1 where I can choose to fail and put evil ahead immediately. Approving reveals little since many players approve round-1 teams, and it helps evil by likely sending me on the quest.
Player 3: I only know my own role as Good; there is no behavioral information yet from quest outcomes or votes. The current phase is team voting on round 1 for quest 1, and as a Good servant I want an efficient, low-risk team. Since I proposed this exact 2-person team including myself and there is no evidence against player 0 yet, approving is optimal. My vote reveals consistency with my own proposal and gives little exploitable information this early.
Player 4: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 2, and 3 are good. The proposed team [0,3] includes one evil and one good, which is excellent for evil on quest 1 because 0 can fail it. In team voting as Minion, optimal play is to approve teams containing evil, especially early when a failed first quest creates pressure and confusion. My vote itself reveals little; approving a plausible leader-picked team with the leader on it is normal and helps evil more than rejecting.

As a Servant, my goal is to maximize chances of a successful quest while using team votes to preserve good-leaning teams. The proposed team [0,2,3] includes both prior success members plus me, which is a strong, low-risk composition for a 3-person mission. Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,2,3] contains me but not 4. Quest 1 succeeded with team [3,0], which means I chose pass there to preserve cover; that makes 3 look somewhat good publicly, though not confirmed. Voting approve is optimal since I am included and can influence the quest outcome. Approving also looks consistent with a player supporting their own inclusion; rejecting my own team position would be suspicious and needlessly risks losing access to sabotage.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,4] is entirely evil, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids helping evil pass/fail quest 3 and gives minimal harmful info beyond a principled anti-bad-team vote.
Player 3: Two successful quests so far: [3,0] and [0,2,3]. Player 0 is on both successes, making 0 likely Good. Players 2 and 3 are also on successful teams; 4 is untested. For a 2-person Quest 3, [0,4] includes one strong-good read and one unknown. As a Good servant, approving a team with at least one trusted member is reasonable this early, and rejecting without stronger evidence gives little benefit. My vote reveals mild trust in 0 and willingness to test 4, which can help generate info.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate, while 1/2/3 are good. Two quests have already succeeded with zero fails, including a team with 0 on it, so 0 may be gaining trust. This is team voting on quest 3, and as evil I want chances to fail quests while preserving cover. A 2-player team of [0,4] contains both evil players, guaranteeing we can fail this quest if approved. Voting approve is optimal; approving also aligns with expected behavior since I am on the team, and rejecting would needlessly hurt evil's chance to score.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and both prior successful quests included either me or only good players, with no fails played, which keeps suspicion relatively low on me and 4. We are in team voting on quest 3, and as evil I want a team containing both evil players approved because on a 2-person quest a single fail is enough to cause mission failure. Approving maximizes evil's chance to get on the board immediately. My vote reveals little since leaders often support their own teams, and getting this team through helps evil far more than any suspicion cost.
Player 2: Two successful quests give some goodwill to players on those teams: quest 1 had 3 and 0; quest 2 had 0,2,3. So 0 and 3 look fairly good, and I know I am good, which makes quest 2 fully good if no deception occurred; with two evils in 5-player Avalon and no incentive to hide twice, 0 and 3 are strong good candidates. Player 4 is less cleared because this is their first quest appearance. As a good servant in team voting, I should prefer teams containing cleared players. Proposed team [0,4] drops both me and player 3 in favor of uncertain 4, which is unnecessarily risky for quest 3. Rejecting pushes for a safer team like [0,2] or [0,3]. My vote signals skepticism of introducing an untested player when we already have successful options, which helps good coordination.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate; quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with no fails despite including me on quest 1, so I have some cover. Current phase is quest voting on quest 3, and the team is exactly both evil players [0,4]. As evil, the optimal play is to sabotage now to prevent good from reaching 3 successes and to create uncertainty; with two evil on team, at least one fail guarantees the quest fails. My fail reveals that at least one of {0,4} is evil, which is acceptable because letting good go to 3-0 would be much worse.
Player 4: I know player 0 is evil with me. Quest 1 had team [3,0] and succeeded with 0 fails, so 0 hid evil then. Quest 2 had [0,2,3] and also succeeded with 0 fails, again likely intentional restraint by 0 to avoid early detection. Now on quest 3, team [0,4] has both evil players. As evil on a 5-player game, reaching 1 fail here is very strong because it puts score at 2-1 and confirms suspicion on this tiny team, but since both evils are present, only one fail is needed and double-failing is unnecessary. From my perspective, failing is optimal because letting a third success through would immediately lose the game for evil. The information revealed by a fail hurts these specific players' cover, but we cannot afford another success; the strategic necessity is to sabotage.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Quest 3 team [0,4] produced 2 fail votes, so both 0 and 4 are certainly evil in a 5-player game. Therefore the good players are 1,2,3. The proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both confirmed evils and should guarantee a success on quest 4. Approving also reveals consistent good voting and helps my team lock the game.
Player 4: I know 0 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [0,4] got 2 fails, fully outing both of us to observant players. Team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils, so it is very likely all-good and would succeed, putting Good to 3 wins and likely lose the game for Evil. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject any clean team likely to pass. My vote revealing opposition to an all-good team is acceptable because we are already highly suspicious from the double-fail quest.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Team [0,4] produced 2 fails, hard-confirming both of us as evil to attentive players, so avoiding either of us on quest can help good. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both evils, so it is very likely all-good and would succeed, putting good to 3-1 and near victory. As evil on a team vote, optimal play is to reject strong all-good teams. My reject also aligns with preserving ambiguity only slightly, but mainly prevents a likely good success.
Player 3: I’m Good. From quest history: [3,0] succeeded and [0,2,3] succeeded, so 0,2,3 looked good then. But [0,4] on a 2-person quest produced 2 fail votes, which conclusively means both 0 and 4 are Evil. Therefore 1,2,3 is an all-Good team and should be approved. Approving also helps lock in the winning 3-person core for quest 4.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils, making it the strongest possible 3-person team for quest 4. Previous fail on [0,4] confirms both are evil; prior successes with [3,0] and [0,2,3] imply 2 and 3 are good since 0 can choose to pass. As good/Merlin, I should approve this safe team. Approving also aligns with a natural good vote and does not reveal extra Merlin-specific info beyond supporting a clean team.
